summary.category: The Cartesian System and Coordinate Axes
points: The Cartesian system uses two perpendicular coordinate axes—the horizontal X-axis and the vertical Y-axis—intersecting at the origin O(0, 0) to locate any point in a plane using an ordered pair of coordinates (x, y).
The coordinate axes divide the plane into four quadrants: Quadrant I (+, +), II (−, +), III (−, −), and IV (+, −), numbered anticlockwise from the positive x-axis.

The x-coordinate (abscissa) measures perpendicular distance from the Y-axis, and the y-coordinate (ordinate) measures perpendicular distance from the X-axis; points on axes have either x or y equal to zero.

Every point in the Cartesian plane is uniquely identified by its coordinates (x, y), where (x, y) ≠ (y, x) unless x = y, establishing a one-to-one correspondence between points and ordered pairs of real numbers.

The universal convention of this coordinate system, developed by René Déscartes, allows consistent and unambiguous description of point locations across mathematics, science, and real-world applications.
